sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 14, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Two 3×3 green tokens with colored centers (white=active, charcoal=inactive). Arrow keys move the active token in 3-cell steps. MOUSE on a center swaps activation. Purple wall blocks movement. Two containers on left and right platforms. Goal: place tokens into containers.
- Plan: Move the active right token UP over the purple wall, RIGHT past it, then DOWN into the right container.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 13 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Important finding from the first UP transition:
- Before: left token at [30,21]-[32,23], right token at [30,27]-[32,29]
- After: left token at [27,21]-[29,23], right token at [30,27]-[32,29]
So the FIRST UP move actually moved the LEFT token (the inactive one with charcoal center), not the right token! This contradicts my earlier understanding that the white-center token is the active one.
Wait, but at that time (earlier in the game), the left token had the white center and the right token had the black center. Let me re-check. The first UP was done early in the game when the left token had the white center.
But now, after I clicked on the right token's center, the right token has the white center and the left token has the charcoal center. And the 4 UP moves I just did were all no_impact, meaning the right token (with white center) couldn't move UP.
So the question is: which token is actually controlled by arrow keys? Let me think about this more carefully:
1. Initially, left token had white center, right token had black center.
2. I moved LEFT token around with arrow keys (it moved).
3. I clicked on right token's center → left token's center became charcoal, right token's center became white.
4. I tried RIGHT → right token moved from [30,27]-[32,29] to [30,30]-[32,32].
5. I tried RIGHT again → blocked (no_impact).
6. I tried UP x4 → all no_impact (right token blocked from moving UP).
So the right token CAN move RIGHT but not UP. This suggests the token is constrained to move only horizontally on its current row. But wait, earlier the left token moved UP. Let me check if the left token can still move.
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Left token: [30,21]-[32,23] with charcoal center
- Right token: [30,30]-[32,32] with white center
The right token is on the gap bridge [30,24]-[32,29] extended area. It can move LEFT back onto the bridge and onto the left platform, or RIGHT into the purple wall (blocked).
Wait, but the 4 UP moves were no_impact. This could mean:
1. The right token can't move UP (blocked by something above)
2. Or the active token is actually the one with the charcoal center (left token), and it's blocked from moving UP
Let me check: the left token is at [30,21]-[32,23] on the left platform [21,9]-[41,23]. If I try to move it UP, it would go to [27,21]-[29,23], which is still on the platform. So the left token should be able to move UP.
But the 4 UP moves were no_impact. This suggests the left token is NOT the active one currently. The active one is the right token (white center), and it's blocked from moving UP.
Why is the right token blocked from moving UP? Let me check what's above it. The right token is at [30,30]-[32,32]. Above it would be rows 27-29, cols 30-32. This area is part of the gray background (the gap between platforms). There's no platform there, so maybe the token can only move on platforms?
Actually, looking at the adjacency list: the right token (8) is adjacent to the gap bridge (7) and the purple wall (2). The gap bridge is at [30,24]-[32,29]. So the token is on the gray background, adjacent to the bridge.
Hmm, maybe the token can only move along the bridge horizontally, not vertically. Let me test by moving LEFT to see if the token can go back onto the left platform.
Actually, I need to reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe:
- The token can only move on connected white/gray surfaces
- UP from [30,30]-[32,32] would go to [27,30]-[29,32], which is on the gray background but not connected to any platform
- So the token is blocked because there's no platform above
Let me try moving LEFT to get back to the left platform, then test if the token can move there.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model revised: The active token (white center) can only move on connected platform surfaces. The right token is on the gap bridge and can't move UP because there's no platform above. Need to move LEFT back to platforms to navigate vertically.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move LEFT to get the right token back to the left platform
# Current: [30,30]-[32,32], need to go LEFT to [30,27]-[32,29] then [30,24]-[32,26]... 
# Actually let's just try LEFT and see where it goes
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
